question_frequency.rationale: The Pair posed a substantive didactic question at every meaningful decision point: what to binary search on (Turn 1), sentinel choice (Turn 2), and adjustment-direction equivalence (Turn 3). Three distinct teachable forks across a 4-turn solve is high density.

question_quality.rationale: Each fork hit a genuine crux: partition-index vs value-search (the central algorithmic insight), ±inf vs arithmetic sentinels (correctness with floats), and the equivalence of the two invalidity checks (subtle but real). No cosmetic choices.

scaffolding.rationale: Progression flowed naturally — design (what to search on) → conceptual scaffolding (partition picture, `(m+n+1)//2`) → implementation detail (sentinels) → loop logic (adjustment direction) → full code. Difficulty ramped smoothly with no spoilers; the Pair waited for the user's reasoning before confirming.

flow_and_tone.rationale: Tone was warm and the back-and-forth felt collaborative, with the Pair acknowledging the user's reasoning and building on it. Minor friction: the Pair stumbled on the unavailable options tool at the start ("The options tool isn't available..."), which leaked plumbing into the conversation, though it recovered cleanly.

effectiveness.rationale: Final code is correct, passes all the same edge cases as the baseline (empty arrays, duplicates, odd/even totals), and achieves O(log(min(m,n))) / O(1) — functionally equivalent to baseline.

friction.rationale: 4 turns / 150s vs baseline's 1 turn / 43s — roughly 3.5x longer, but the user co-derived the solution rather than receiving it, with three substantive design discussions. The teaching payoff is proportional to the extra length, and the user explicitly noted the value of the design-first framing.
